coredata.dc:title: Preparation of high-yield carbon quantum dots and paper-based sensors from biomass wastes by mechano-chemical method
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Hong-Chao
Chen, Shuo
Tan, Zi-Xiang
Jiang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from biomass through a "bottom-up" method has attracted a great deal of interest because of its outstanding environmental and economic advantages. However, the yield of biomass-based CQDs is still low due to the high polymerization of biochar. In this study, we propose a new approach for harvesting high-yield biomass-based CQDs by integrating mechanochemistry and strong chemical oxidation. Ball milling can reduce the particle size and resistance of biochar and increase the contents of CC, while the powerful oxidant HOO- produced by alkaline hydrogen peroxide can break CC to generate biomass-based CQDs. Using this two-step process, the yields of biomass-based CQDs reached 57.75% for blue-green algae, 65.8% for straw, 47.9% for corn protein meal, and 45.35% for Typha angustifolia. Based on the excellent sensitivity and selectivity for Fe3+ of biomass-based CQDs, CQDs paper-based sensors were prepared for expedient and precise detection of Fe3+ in surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent, and tap water. When the Fe3+ content is between 0 and 60 μM, the R2 of Fe3+ detection by blue-green algae CQDs can reach 0.9956. This work provides a new and reliable method for the large-scale preparation of biomass-based CQDs and expands their environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ball milling
Carbon quantum dots
Alkaline peroxide
Biomass
Paper-based sensors
Fe3+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Yield and formation mechanism of BCQDs
Mechanism analysis of high yield of BCQDs
Preparation of paper-based sensors and application to real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Research and Development Plan of China Tobacco
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Key Research and Development Plan of China Tobacco (No. 110202202014), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (22076178).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of BCQDs, (b) FT-IR spectra of BCQDs, (c) Total XPS spectra, and (d) C1s spectra of BCQDs.
Effect of (a) the time of ball milling, (b)the speed of ball milling, (c) H2O2 and NaOH dosage on the yield of BCQDs and (d) the yield of BCQDs with different preparation methods.
SEM of bluegreen algae (a) before and (b)after ball milling, (c) resistance, (d) FT-IR and (e) XPS images of bluegreen algae before and after ball milling and BCQDs.

PL emission spectra and UVvis absorption spectra of BCQDs prepared by different preparations: (a) bluegreen algae, (b) ball-milled bluegreen algae, (c) ball-milled bluegreen algae+ H2O2, (d) ball-milled bluegreen algae+ NaOH, (e) bluegreen algae + H2O2/NaOH and (f) ball-milled bluegreen algae+ H2O2/NaOH.
PL intensity (a) at different pH values and (b) continuous radiation with a 286 W Xe lamp.

(a) Fluorescence response of BCQDs in the presence of different ions, (b) fluorescence response of BCQDs in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations, (c) Stern-Volmer plot of Fe3+ concentration, (d) images of BCQDs and BCQDs+Fe3+ solutions under 365 nm UV irradiation.

(a) Preparation and use of paper-based sensor, (b) standard card of paper-based sensor for Fe3+ detection, (c) different Fe3+ detection images in surface water (top), wastewater treatment plant effluent (middle) and tap water (bottom), (d) Fe3+ detection in sludge treatment wastewater.

row.entry: carbon quantum dots preparation from blue-green algae biochar
ball milling and H2O2 co-treatment of biochar
ball milling and NaOH co-treatment of biochar
H2O2 and NaOH co-treatment of biochar
ball milling and H2O2/NaOH co-treatment of biochar

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Yields of CQDs Prepared from Various Biomasses.

head.ce:title: Preparation of high-yield carbon quantum dots and paper-based sensors from biomass wastes by mechano-chemical method

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from biomass through a "bottom-up" method has attracted a great deal of interest because of its outstanding environmental and economic advantages. However, the yield of biomass-based CQDs is still low due to the high polymerization of biochar. In this study, we propose a new approach for harvesting high-yield biomass-based CQDs by integrating mechanochemistry and strong chemical oxidation. Ball milling can reduce the particle size and resistance of biochar and increase the contents of CC, while the powerful oxidant HOO- produced by alkaline hydrogen peroxide can break CC to generate biomass-based CQDs. Using this two-step process, the yields of biomass-based CQDs reached 57.75% for blue-green algae, 65.8% for straw, 47.9% for corn protein meal, and 45.35% for Typha angustifolia. Based on the excellent sensitivity and selectivity for Fe3+ of biomass-based CQDs, CQDs paper-based sensors were prepared for expedient and precise detection of Fe3+ in surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent, and tap water. When the Fe3+ content is between 0 and 60 μM, the R2 of Fe3+ detection by blue-green algae CQDs can reach 0.9956. This work provides a new and reliable method for the large-scale preparation of biomass-based CQDs and expands their environmental applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial [1], CQDs have recently become attractive nanomaterial candidates for scientific and industrial applications because of their excellent photoluminescence properties, high chemical stability, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, water solubility, and environmental friendliness [2–5]. These excellent characteristics make CQDs have potential applications in fluorescence sensing, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, biological imaging, drug delivery, and other fields [6–10]. Researchers have also developed a series of methods to synthesize CQDs, such as laser irradiation, electrochemistry, arc discharge, plasma treatment, and hydrothermal/acidic oxidation [11–14]. However, most CQDs are produced from nonrenewable resources, such as citric acid, urea, ethylenediamine, monoethanolamine, and acetic acid [15–19]. Conventional preparation method not only consumes resources but also may bring a series of environmental problems.

Renewable organic solid wastes (i.e., agricultural wastes and wetland plant waste) are abundant in the world [20,21]. In China, the total amount of biomass wastes is likely to reach 1.527 billion t⋅a−1 in 2018 [22]. Using biomass waste to prepare CQDs should be a promising strategy for disposing of the biomass wastes and simultaneously recovering value-added products [23]. The synthesis of CQDs from solid waste biomass has recently attracted many researchers’ attention due to its low cost and green properties [24]. For instance, researchers have synthesized CQDs using banana juice, grass, rice husk, soybeans, eggs, pigeon feathers, and feces [25–29]. However, the yield of CQDs from biomass is still low [24]. For instance, the yields of CQDs prepared by hydrothermal and pyrolysis methods only reached 7.706% and 10%, respectively [30,31].

Ball milling, a mechanochemical process, can not only reduce the particle size of carbon materials but also introduce abundant defects and oxygen-containing functional groups [32,33]. Simultaneously, alkaline hydrogen peroxide treatment can produce strongly oxidizing HOO- that can destroy unsaturated structures such as conjugated double bonds and carbonyl groups in lignin and introduce carboxyl groups [34]. Combining both mechanochemical and chemical oxidation treatments may dramatically elevate the yield of biomass-based CQDs and improve the quality of the biomass-based CQDs. Therefore, in the present study, we propose an innovative preparation method for CQDs from biomass by combining mechanical ball milling and chemical oxidation. The two-step bond-breaking process made the as-prepared biomass-based CQDs have a uniform size and excellent optical properties with high yield. To verify the versatility of this method, typical biomasses, such as blue-green algae, straw, corn protein meal, and Typha angustifolia, were used to prepare biomass-based CQDs. Meanwhile, iron ions are widely existed in surface water and groundwater, which may bring harm to humans, so quick and convenient detection of iron ions in water bodies is meaningful [35–37]. To expand the application of biomass-based CQDs, CQDs paper-based sensors for detecting Fe3+ were prepared and can test easily and quickly in different water samples, i.e., surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent, and tap water. This method is expected to be used for the large-scale preparation of biomass-based CQDs and has greater prospects for application in the environmental field.

Bluegreen algae were gathered from Chaohu Lake in Anhui province, China. Typha angustifolia was harvested from the University of Science and Technology of China; straw and corn gluten meal were purchased from a local factory near in China. H2O2, NaOH, HCl, and nitrates were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. These reagents are analytically pure and do not require any treatment before use. Biotech cellulose ester membrane (MWCO 500D) was purchased from Hefei Dubaier Chemical Technology Co., Ltd., China.

First, the blue-green algae were washed in water and then dried at 80 °C overnight. Then, the dried blue-green algae were pulverized with a pulverizer and passed through a 100-mesh sieve to obtain a blue-green algal powder. The blue-green algal powder was pyrolyzed at a heating rate of 5 °C at 250 °C in a nitrogen gas atmosphere for 2 h to obtain biochar.

First, 0.2 g of biochar and ten 10 mm and fifty 6 mm agate balls (total volume of approximately 11 mL) were filled into a 100 mL agate ball mill jar. After that, the ball milling jars were ball milled at 180 rpm for 1 h, 2 h and 3 h to investigate the effect of ball milling time and at 90 rpm, 180 rpm and 270 rpm for 2 h to investigate the effect of ball milling speed. The biochar in the ball mill jar was washed out with H2O (99 mL, 98 mL, 97 mL), then H2O2 (1 mL, 2 mL, 3 mL) and NaOH (0.1 g, 0.2 g, 0.3 g) were added, and the solution was stirred at 1600 rpm and room temperature for 8 h to obtain a solution rich in quantum dots. The solution was neutralized with 2 M HCl, and afterward, the solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm to obtain the supernatant. Then, the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μM organic filter. The solution after filtration was poured into a 500D dialysis bag. The dialysis bag was immersed in 1000 mL of deionized water with continuous stirring, and the water was replaced every 6 h until no fluorescent material was detected. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried to obtain blue-green algae carbon quantum dots (BCQDs). Store the quantum dot powder at 4 °C until use. The CQDs were prepared from corn protein meal, straw, and Typha angustifolia in the same way as the BCQDs, except that blue-green algae were replaced with the corresponding biomass in the preparation of biochar. Table 1 is an abbreviation list of terms in the preparation process. Fig. S1 illustrates the variation in the preparation of BCQDs from blue-green algae.

The UV−vis absorption spectra were recorded by a 6100 s spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The crystal structure of the BCQDs was characterized at 2θ values from 10° to 70° by using X-ray diffraction (XRD) equipment. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was conducted on an Equinox 55 IR spectrometer with a detection range of 4000–400 cm−1. The chemical states of the elements were analyzed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to examine the morphologies of the biochar. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to test the structures and sizes of the BCQDs.

To test the selectivity of BCQDs to Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of solutions containing 50 mg/L BCQDs and 500 μM of different ions (Fe2+、Ag+、Cu2+、Al3+、Bi+、Cd2+、Co2+、Mg2+、Ni2+、Zn2+、Cr3+、Ca2+、Fe3+) was recorded at 360 nm excitation wavelength. To test the sensitivity of BCQDs to Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of solutions containing 50 mg/L BCQDs and 0–150 μM Fe3+ was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

BCQDs (5 mg) were dissolved in 100 mL of water. A circle filter paper was soaked in the above solution for 20 min. Then, the filter paper was removed and allowed to dry naturally. Immerse the prepared filter paper in solutions of different Fe3+ concentrations for approximately 5 s, irradiate the test paper with a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, and obtain a standard card for the Fe3+ concentration according to the fluorescence color of the test paper.

The process of preparing BCQDs from blue-green algae is shown in Fig. S2. BCQDs prepared by this method have good monodispersity and homogeneity ( Fig. 1a). They have an average particle size of 1.53 nm, and the particle size distribution of BCQDs is mainly distributed in a narrow range of 1–2.3 nm. The FT-IR spectral analysis (Fig. 1b) demonstrated that the BCQDs contained rich oxygen-containing functional groups, including C-OH (1088 cm−1) [38], -COOH (1380 cm−1) [39], and CO (1640 cm−1) [40], and these hydrophilic groups ensured that the BCQDs had good dispersion in water. The full-range XPS analysis (Fig. 1c) clearly showed the characteristic peaks of C1s at 284.8 eV, N1s at 399.9 eV, and O1s at 531.7 eV of the BCQDs at 66.7%, 7.2%, and 24.6% [39], respectively. Among them, the N content in the BCQDs is almost unchanged relative to the 6.9% N content in the blue-green algae biochar (Table S1), which proves that this preparation method can retain the N element in blue-green algae well. The high-resolution C1s spectrum has five main peaks (Fig. 1d), and the characteristic peaks at 284.5 and 284.9 eV confirm the presence of graphite or aliphatic structures [41]. The 286 eV binding energy peak indicates the presence of C-N, and the binding energy peaks at 286.8 eV and 288.8 eV demonstrate the presence of C-O and COOH [42], respectively. This suggests that the synthesized BCQDs contain abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. The XRD pattern of the BCQDs (Fig. S3) shows a broad diffraction peak at approximately 22° and 55°, which correspond to peaks 002 and 100 of graphitic carbon, respectively, indicating the presence of graphitic carbon phase. In addition, the presence of amorphous carbon resulted in a broadening of the 002-diffraction peak [43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Yield and formation mechanism of BCQDs

ce:section.ce:para: To verify the effects of ball milling and the amount of NaOH and H2O2 on the yield of BCQDs, we set up a series of condition experiments. Fig. 2a shows that the ball milling time slightly effects the yield of BCQDs, and there is no obvious increase in yield when the ball milling time is 3 h, so the optimal ball milling time is 2 h. Fig. 2b shows the effect of the rotate speed of ball milling on the yield of BCQDs. When the rotate speed was 180 rpm, the yield of BCQDs reached the maximum, and then decreased when the rotate speed of the ball milling further increased. Mitra’s study showed that the average particle size of biochar increased with increasing rotate speed at lower ball milling times, which may be caused by the increased aggregation of biochar particles [45]. The dosages of H2O2 and NaOH also influenced the yield of BCQDs (Fig. 2c). When the dosages of H2O2 and NaOH were 2 mL and 0.2 g, respectively, the yield reached maximum. The yield decreased when the further increased dosage of H2O2 and NaOH, probably because the molecular weight of BCQDs became too small due to the excess of HOO-, which would escape during the dialysis process.

As shown in Fig. 2d, we found that both BMBA and BMBA+H2O2 could only slightly enhance the yield of BCQDs, while BMBA+ NaOH could markedly enhance the yield of BCQDs, probably because the alkaline washing effect of NaOH solution could destroy the surface structure of biochar and strip out some small-sized biochar from the surface of biochar [46]. The XRD of BCQDs prepared by BMBA+H2O2 and BMBA+ NaOH are shown in Fig. S4. The peak intensity of BMBA+ NaOH is higher than that of BMBA+H2O2, which may be attributed to the higher degree of graphitization of the samples [47]. However, the dispersibility and fluorescence properties of BCQDs prepared by alkaline washing with NaOH solution were poor in water. We found that H2O2 (adding 2 mL of H2O2 to the solution after NaOH alkaline washing and stirring for 8 h) could obviously improve the dispersibility and fluorescence properties of BCQDs obtained by alkaline washing with NaOH solution alone. As we can see from Fig. S5, the BCQDs are uniformly dispersed in water and no precipitation appears after H2O2 treatment, and their fluorescence intensity is higher than that of the BCQDs obtained by BMBA+ NaOH alone. Furthermore, the H2O2-treated biochar solution was a yellow-brown solution, which was different from the black solution obtained by alkaline washing alone, suggesting a change in BCQDs properties (Fig. S6).

The TEM images of BCQDs prepared by BMBA+H2O2, BMBA+ NaOH, and BMBA+ H2O2/NaOH are shown in Fig. S7. All three BCQDs have uniform particle sizes. The average particle sizes of the BCQDs prepared by BMBA+H2O2, BMBA + NaOH, and BMBA+ H2O2/NaOH are 4.18, 1.64, and 1.53 nm, respectively. The average particle sizes of the BCQDs prepared by BMBA and BA+H2O2/NaOH are 1.99 and 1.84 nm, respectively (Fig. S8). BCQDs prepared by BMBA alone have relatively large particle sizes. BCQDs prepared by BMBA+ H2O2 have the largest particle size, which may be due to the aggregation of small particles caused by the O2 bubbles produced by the decomposition of H2O2 [48]. BCQDs prepared by BMBA+ NaOH have smaller particle sizes due to alkali washing. BCQDs prepared by BA+H2O2/NaOH have relatively small particle sizes, which may be caused by alkali washing and strong oxidation of HOO-. The BCQDs prepared by BMBA+H2O2/NaOH have the smallest particle size, which may be caused by the combined interaction of increasing CC content by ball milling, alkali washing, and HOO- oxidation (Section 3.2.2 has a detailed description). These results suggest that combining ball milling and H2O2/NaOH allows the BCQDs to have the smallest average particle size.

The XRD spectra of the three BCQDs (BMBA, BA+H2O2/NaOH, and BMBA + H2O2/NaOH)) are shown in Fig. S9(a). The diffraction peaks located at 20°− 25° and 55° correspond to the 002 and 100 diffraction peaks of graphitic carbon, respectively. The FT-IR images are shown in Fig. S9(b). All three BCQDs have more oxygen-containing functional groups. Among them, the BCQDs prepared by BMBA had more phenolic hydroxyl groups, the BCQDs prepared by BA+H2O2/NaOH had less hydroxyl functional groups, and the BCQDs prepared by BMBA + H2O2/NaOH had more alcohol hydroxyl groups. These results suggest that combing ball milling and chemical oxidation processes can obtain BCQDs with smaller particle sizes and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups.

The yields of CQDs prepared from biochar of different biomasses by different methods are listed in Table 2. The yields of CQDs obtained by our method are several times higher than those obtained by conventional hydrothermal, ultrasonic, and plasma induction methods. Furthermore, the two-step balling-oxidation method can extend to other biomasses, i.e., the yields of CQDs reached 57.75% for blue-green algae, 65.8% for straw, 47.9% for corn protein meal and 45.35% for Typha angustifolia. Based on the TEM images, the average particle sizes of the CQDs of blue-green algae, corn protein meal, straw, and Typha angustifolia are 1.53, 3.22, 1.57, and 1.72 nm, respectively (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of high yield of BCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the effects of ball milling, H2O2, and NaOH on the BCQDs yield, some characterizations were performed. The results indicated that the ball milling treatment markedly changed the morphology, surface crystal structure, and elemental composition of the biochar. SEM images ( Fig. 3a-b) show that the particle size of biochar decreases and becomes more uniform after ball milling compared to the original biochar. This can obviously increase the reaction area of biochar with NaOH solution and HOO-. Meanwhile, we investigated the impact of ball milling on the electron transfer capacity of biochar using EIS. Fig. 3c shows that ball-milling-treated blue-green algae biochar has a smaller semicircle diameter, indicating that ball milling treatment can substantially decrease the electron transfer resistance of blue-green algae biochar. This results in a higher electron transfer capacity of biochar and therefore facilitates the reaction of HOO- with the unsaturated functional groups of the biochar, thus increasing the yield of BCQDs.

FT-IR plots (Fig. 3d) before and after ball milling reveal that ball milling can significantly increase the aromatic C-H (762 cm−1) [56], C-OH (1088 cm−1) and CC (1454 cm−1) [57] of biochar, which may be due to the formation of radical pools on the material surface induced by ball milling, leading to the formation of reactive carbon species and promoting the binding of oxygen species to increase their oxygen content [32]. Xiao et al. found that ball milling can increase the CC and C-OH functional group content of biochar [57]. In contrast, the aromatic C-H, CC, and C-OH decreased significantly, and -COOH increased obviously after the addition of NaOH and H2O2. These results suggest that the aromatic unsaturated functional groups of blue-green algae are disrupted to generate hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups of the BCQDs.

The results of the XPS analysis (Fig. 3e) indicated that the CC content of blue-green algae increased from 18.55% to 25.40% after ball milling, while the C-O amount increased from 16.07% to 20.16%. After treatment with NaOH and H2O2, the CC amount decreased abruptly from 25.40% to 17.89%, while the COOH amount increased from 8.17% to 12.97%, which is the same as the FT-IR characterization. These results also indicate that the HOO- generated by H2O2 with strong oxidizing properties promoted the breakage of unsaturated structures and was oxidized to COOH.

Based on the above analysis, we can conclude that ball milling and alkaline hydrogen peroxide can promote the yield of BCQDs. First, ball milling reduces the size of biochar and increases the specific surface area while creating more reactive sites and accelerating the reaction rate. Meanwhile, ball milling forms more unsaturated CC structures on the surface of biochar through mechanochemical action, which creates more reaction sites for HOO-. Then, NaOH and H2O2 generate HOO- with strong oxidizing properties through the reaction of H2O2 + OH- → H2O + HOO- [58]. The HOO- can not only greatly improve the dispersibility and fluorescence properties of BCQDs washed down by alkali, but also react with unsaturated functional groups of the biochar and promote the oxidation of biochar to break the bonds to generate BCQDs.

To investigate the optical characteristics of BCQDs obtained by different preparation methods, we investigated the PL emission spectra and UVvis absorption spectra of BCQDs in detail. As shown in Fig. S11, the BCQDs obtained by different preparation methods showed absorption peaks at 250, 272, and 340 nm. The absorption peak at 250 nm is typical for the n-p * transition or the aromatic π system for the carbonyl [59]. The second absorption peak at 272 nm is the n → π * transition for the carbonyl. The third absorption peak at approximately 340 nm also originates from the n → π * edge transition, which is associated with the edge of the BCQDs nucleus and is responsible for the bright luminescence of the main emission center [60]. The absorption of BCQDs is gradually enhanced with the change in preparation method, which is positively correlated with the increase in its yield, indicating that the increase in yield also provides more emission centers for BCQDs. As shown in Fig. 4, we found that the emission peaks gradually move to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength increases, indicating that the photoluminescence properties of BCQDs depend on the excitation wavelength and have excitation-dependent properties [61]. As the excitation wavelength changes from 340 nm to 380 nm in Fig. 4f, the emission wavelength of the BCQDs increases from 434 nm to 449 nm. This narrow photoluminescence emission region indicates that the size of the BCQDs is uniform, which is consistent with the TEM results (Fig. 1a). Li's study also showed that different sizes of CQDs have large disparities in PL spectra due to size effects, which also proved that the BCQDs synthesized in this work have a uniform size [62]. As shown in Fig. S12, all CQDs prepared from four kinds of biomasses have strong UV absorbance at 250, 272, and 340 nm, indicating that all four CQDs have more emission centers. Their PL spectra show that the emission wavelength increases with the increasing excitation wavelength, indicating that the four CQDs also have excitation-dependent properties.

To validate the stability of BCQDs, we tested the fluorescence intensity of BCQDs under different conditions. The initial pH of a 50 mg/L solution of BCQDs is approximately 6.8. Fig. 5a shows the impact of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the BCQDs solution over a wide pH range (3−12). The results show that the change in the BCQDs solution varies negligibly in the pH range of 3–11, which proves that the BCQDs solution is stable in this wide pH range and can work under approximate environmental conditions. In addition, we verified the ambient light stability of the BCQDs solution by irradiating it with UV lamps. As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity of the BCQDs solution reduced by only 7% after 24 h of irradiation with a 286 nm UV lamp. These results prove that the BCQDs solution has stable fluorescence performance and better environmental adaptation performance.

To validate the potential environmental applications of BCQDs as fluorescent probes, the fluorescence response was tested in the presence of different metal ions. Fig. 6a shows the fluorescence intensity changes of BCQDs in the presence of different metal ions. Most of the metal ions do not cause significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of BCQDs. Compared to other metal ions, Fe3+ can obviously quench the fluorescence intensity of BCQDs to 18.2%. Due to the unique quenching property of Fe3+, BCQDs are highly selective for Fe3+ and thus can be used to test Fe3+. The effect of fluorescence quenching of BCQDs on Fe3+ was investigated, as shown in Fig. 6b. The fluorescence intensity of BCQDs at 444 nm gradually decreased as the Fe3+ amount increased from 0 μM to 150 μM, indicating that BCQDs have a strong sensitivity to Fe3+. Fig. 6c shows that the quenching of BCQDs by Fe3+ follows the Stern-Volmer equation:
(1) I0/I=1+K[C]
where I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of BCQDs at 444 nm in solutions with and without Fe3+, respectively. K is the quenching constant, and [C] is the concentration of Fe3+.

A good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9956) between the Fe3+ contents and fluorescence intensities in the range of 0–60 μM was obtained, which indicates that BCQDs have potential as nanoprobes for Fe3+ detection. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 6d, the fluorescence of the BCQDs solution notably changed after the addition of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper-based sensors and application to real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with the traditional detection method, which has the disadvantages of expensive and precise instruments, CQDs paper-based sensors have the advantages of low cost, portability, simplicity of operation, and rapid detection [63,64]. The BCQDs have a wide detection range of Fe3+, uniform particle size distribution, and stable fluorescence performance (Table S2). Based on these excellent properties of BCQDs, we designed a BCQDs paper-based sensor for Fe3+ detection. As shown in Fig. 7a, we first prepared standard cards for Fe3+ detection in the laboratory. The luminescence intensity of the BCQDs paper-based sensor gradually decreased with increasing Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 7b). This proves that the BCQDs paper-based sensor can be used for the detection of Fe3+ in water bodies. Afterward, we tested the potential application of paper-based sensors in practical applications using surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent, and tap water. Since the Fe3+ contents in surface water, wastewater treatment plant effluent, and tap water are low, we chose to add 20, 40, and 60 μM Fe3+ to these three practical water samples [65]. The solutions were tested by a paper-based sensor, and the results are shown in Fig. 7c. The detection results are consistent with the standard card. Meanwhile, we selected a sludge treatment effluent with high Fe3+ content for practical validation. As shown in Fig. 7d, the Fe3+ content in the wastewater after three dilutions was measured to be 45 μM according to the o-phenanthroline colorimetric method [66], and the measurement results of the paper-based sensor also showed that the Fe3+ content was located between 40 and 50 μM, which indicated the feasibility and reliability of our paper-based sensor in detecting Fe3+ in actual water bodies. This successful application provides a new idea for the future development of portable Fe3+ detection test strips.

In summary, we proposed an environmentally friendly, simple, and large-scale method for preparing biomass-based CQDs by combining mechanochemical and chemical oxidation. At the optimum preparation conditions, the yield of BCQDs reaches 57.75%. Multiple characterizations confirm that the ball milling process markedly reduces the particle size of biochar and the resistance of biochar to electron transfer, meanwhile increasing the content of CC and oxygen-containing functional groups. HOO- generated by alkaline hydrogen peroxide can break the unsaturated functional groups on biochar, such as CC, and obtain BCQDs with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. The proposed method is available for four typical biomass wastes. The prepared BCQDs have excellent optical properties and high selectivity for Fe3+ detection, and their detection R2 can reach 0.9956 when the Fe3+ content is between 0 and 60 μM. A paper-based sensor of BCQDs was prepared and used for Fe3+ detection in practical water bodies. The yield of BCQDs can be further increased by optimizing process parameters and might be extended to other biomass wastes in the future.

Hong-Chao Hao: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Investigation. Hong Jiang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Other authors: Revise, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to inﬂuence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Key Research and Development Plan of China Tobacco (No. 110202202014), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (22076178).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.111406.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wang, L. Wen, L. Zhao, J. Chao, F. Tao, F. Wang, C. Li, Development of fluorescence sensor and test paper based on molecularly imprinted carbon quantum dots for spiked detection of domoic acid in shellfish and lake water, Anal. Chim. Acta., 1197 (2022) 339515.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Turning agroforestry waste into value-added fluorescent carbon quantum dots for effective detection of Fe3+ in an aqueous environment
ACS EST Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ren, F. Qi, A. Labidi, A.A. Allam, J.S. Ajarem, D.W. Bahnemann, C. Wang, Turning Agroforestry Waste into Value-Added Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots for Effective Detection of Fe3+ in an Aqueous Environment, ACS ES&T Eng., 3 (2022) 260–270.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interfacial molecular fractionation on ferrihydrite reduces the photochemical reactivity of dissolved organic matter
Environ. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, J. Lv, S. Zhang, P. Christie, S. Zhang, Interfacial Molecular Fractionation on Ferrihydrite Reduces the Photochemical Reactivity of Dissolved Organic Matter, Environ. Sci. Technol., 55 (2021) 1769–1778.
